text.title: design-tokens-implementation Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for design-tokens-implementation. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Organise tokens in three layers: primitives (raw hex/rem values), semantic (intent-named aliases like bg.surface.default), and component (component-scoped like button.primary.bg). App code must consume only semantic and component layers.
rule.rationale: Consuming primitives directly couples components to raw values. When a brand color changes, only the primitive file and semantic aliases need updating — not every component. The semantic layer is the public contract between design and code.

rule.statement: Never expose primitive tokens to app code. Components must reference semantic and component tokens only.
rule.rationale: Primitive tokens are implementation details. Semantic tokens are the public contract. Separating them enables rebrand work and mode switching without rewriting components.

rule.statement: Store each mode (light, dark, high-contrast, brand) in a separate file (tokens/modes/light.json, dark.json). Never inline multi-mode values as {light: ..., dark: ...} inside a single token entry.
rule.rationale: Inlined modes drift: updating one side silently breaks the other's contrast pair. Separate files make mode changes auditable in PR diffs. Style Dictionary builds each mode into a separate CSS selector or JS export.

rule.statement: Generated files (CSS, JS, XML, Swift) must be in dist/ and listed in .gitignore unless this package IS the published artifact. Rebuild in CI; never hand-edit generated outputs.
rule.rationale: Agents that commit generated CSS alongside source JSON create dirty repo state on the next build. The source of truth is the JSON; outputs are artifacts. CI should be the only place that builds.

rule.statement: For packages published to npm (e.g., @company/design-tokens), commit the dist/ folder so consumers get built artifacts. For internal monorepo usage, add dist/ to .gitignore.
rule.rationale: npm packages ship artifacts; internal monorepos rebuild per project. Make this distinction explicit in your repo's README.

rule.statement: Validate token JSON against a schema in CI before running the build. Catch broken alias references ({color.brand.500} cycles) before they produce cryptic build errors.
rule.rationale: Style Dictionary silently drops or errors on unresolvable references. A JSON schema check fails fast with a readable message. Catch the error early.

rule.statement: For new projects, use the W3C DTCG format ($value, $type). For Tokens Studio exports, add @tokens-studio/sd-transforms to the Style Dictionary pipeline; without it, the build silently drops styles.
rule.rationale: Tokens Studio uses value and type (no $ prefix). Missing the transform adapter produces empty CSS with no error. The $ prefix is the W3C standard and ensures future tool compatibility.

rule.statement: Pin the Style Dictionary major version explicitly. v3 uses formatter: function({dictionary, file}); v4 uses format: ({dictionary, options}) => string. Do not mix APIs.
rule.rationale: The API breaking change between v3 and v4 is silent. Agents trained on v3 examples emit broken v4 configs that fail at build time. Pinning the version prevents accidental upgrade breakage.

rule.statement: CSS custom properties output must include all semantic tokens at :root and all mode-specific overrides at [data-theme="..."] selectors.
rule.rationale: Web apps toggle [data-theme] attribute to switch modes. CSS custom properties cascade, so dark mode only needs to override changed variables; light values from :root carry over.

rule.statement: Generate Style Dictionary JS output in nested form, then flatten before loading into tailwind.config.js. Do not generate flat output directly; the nested form is reusable across platforms.
rule.rationale: Nested output is platform-agnostic. Flattening at the point of consumption (Tailwind config) keeps the token source versatile.

rule.statement: Configure at least two outputs: CSS for web and JS for tooling/Tailwind. iOS/Android outputs are optional but valuable for multi-platform products.
rule.rationale: CSS custom properties are the standard for web. JavaScript modules are needed for Tailwind and runtime consumption in JS. Native platform outputs keep mobile teams in sync with web design.
